items.summary: Ethan Mollick shares GLSL shader code (hash/noise/fbm functions) generated as part of an AI capability demonstration.
items.reasoning: Concrete artifact of AI code generation but largely a raw code dump with limited interpretive value.
items.summary_html: Ethan Mollick shares GLSL shader code (hash/noise/fbm functions) generated as part of an AI capability demonstration.
items.content_html:
Shader:

precision highp float;

uniform vec2 resolution;

uniform float time;

uniform vec2 mouse;

// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Hash / noise

// ------------------------------------------------------------

float hash12(vec2 p) {

return fract(sin(dot(p, vec2(127.1, 311.7))) * 43758.5453);

}

vec2 hash22(vec2 p) {

p = vec2(dot(p, vec2(127.1, 311.7)),

dot(p, vec2(269.5, 183.3)));

return fract(sin(p) * 43758.5453);

}

float noise(vec2 p) {

vec2 i = floor(p);

vec2 f = fract(p);

float a = hash12(i);

float b = hash12(i + vec2(1.0, 0.0));

float c = hash12(i + vec2(0.0, 1.0));

float d = hash12(i + vec2(1.0, 1.0));

vec2 u = f * f * (3.0 - 2.0 * f);

return mix(a, b, u.x) + (c - a) * u.y * (1.0 - u.x) + (d - b) * u.x * u.y;

}

float fbm(vec2 p) {

float v = 0.0, a = 0.5;

mat2 m = mat2(1.6, 1.2, -1.2, 1.6);

for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) {

v += a * noise(p);

p = m * p;

a *= 0.5;

}

return v;

}

// ------------------------------------------------------------

// SDFs

// ------------------------------------------------------------

float sdBox(vec3 p, vec3 b) {

vec3 q = abs(p) - b;

return length(max(q, 0.0)) + min(max(q.x, max(q.y, q.z)), 0.0);

}

float sdOcta(vec3 p, float s) {

p = abs(p);

return (p.x + p.y + p.z - s) * 0.57735027;

}

// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Light tower (kept simple)

// ------------------------------------------------------------

float tower(vec3 p, vec2 id, float hMul) {

float d = 1e5;

float H = 18.0 + 30.0 * hMul;

float W = 2.8 + 0.9 * hash12(id + 1.7);

// foundations

d = min(d, sdBox(p - vec3(0.0, -10.0, 0.0), vec3(W + 2.2, 12.0, W + 2.2)));

// main shaft (two steps)

d = min(d, sdBox(p - vec3(0.0, H * 0.32, 0.0), vec3(W, H * 0.32, W)));

d = min(d, sdBox(p - vec3(0.0, H * 0.68, 0.0), vec3(W * 0.72, H * 0.28, W * 0.72)));

// cornices

d = min(d, sdBox(p - vec3(0.0, H * 0.28, 0.0), vec3(W + 0.55, 0.25, W + 0.55)));

d = min(d, sdBox(p - vec3(0.0, H * 0.55, 0.0), vec3(W * 0.8 + 0.4, 0.22, W * 0.8 + 0.4)));

// central spire

d = min(d, sdBox(p - vec3(0.0, H + 3.0, 0.0), vec3(0.9, 4.5, 0.9)));

d = min(d, sdOcta(p - vec3(0.0, H + 9.0, 0.0), 3.4));

d = min(d, sdBox(p - vec3(0.0, H + 12.5, 0.0), vec3(0.2, 2.5, 0.2)));

// 4 corner turrets (simple)

for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) {

float a = float(i) * 1.5708 + 0.7854;

vec3 o = vec3(cos(a), 0.0, sin(a)) * (W * 0.95);

vec3 tp = p - o;

float tH = H * 0.55 + 5.0;

d = min(d, sdBox(tp - vec3(0.0, tH * 0.45, 0.0), vec3(0.95, tH * 0.45, 0.95)));

d = min(d, sdOcta(tp - vec3(0.0, tH + 1.8, 0.0), 1.8));

}

// tiny buttresses

for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) {

float a = float(i) * 1.5708;

vec3 dir = vec3(cos(a), 0.0, sin(a));

d = min(d, sdBox(p - dir * (W + 1.6) - vec3(0.0, H * 0.28, 0.0),

vec3(0.55, H * 0.28, 0.55)));

}

return d * 0.9;

}

float city(vec3 p) {

float cell = 28.0;

vec2 id = floor(p.xz / cell);

vec3 q = p;

q.xz = mod(p.xz + 0.5 * cell, cell) - 0.5 * cell;

// slight jitter

q.xz -= (hash22(id) - 0.5) * 5.0;

float hm = pow(hash12(id), 0.6);

float d = tower(q, id, hm);

// occasional second lower tower

if (hash12(id + 9.0) > 0.72) {

vec2 o2 = (hash22(id + 3.1) - 0.5) * 9.0;

d = min(d, tower(q - vec3(o2.x, 0.0, o2.y), id + 20.0, hm * 0.7));

}

// rare big cathedral

if (hash12(id * 0.7) > 0.92) {

d = min(d, tower(q * vec3(1.1, 1.25, 1.1), id + 50.0, 1.05));

}

return d;

}

// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Ocean

// ------------------------------------------------------------

float ocean(vec2 xz) {

float t = time;

float h = 0.0;

h += 2.4 * sin(xz.x * 0.055 + t * 0.55) * cos(xz.y * 0.042 + t * 0.40);

h += 1.8 * sin(xz.y * 0.07 - t * 0.70 + xz.x * 0.02);

h += 1.3 * sin(dot(xz, normalize(vec2(0.75, 0.65))) * 0.10 + t * 0.95);

h += 0.9 * sin(dot(xz, normalize(vec2(-0.55, 0.8))) * 0.14 - t * 1.1);

h += 1.1 * (fbm(xz * 0.06 + t * 0.09) - 0.4);

h += 0.35 * fbm(xz * 0.32 + vec2(t * 0.2, -t * 0.15));

return h;

}

// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Scene

// ------------------------------------------------------------

float map(vec3 p) {

return min(city(p), p.y - ocean(p.xz));

}

float mapB(vec3 p) { return city(p); }

vec3 nBuild(vec3 p) {

vec2 e = vec2(0.02, 0.0);

return normalize(vec3(

mapB(p + e.xyy) - mapB(p - e.xyy),

mapB(p + e.yxy) - mapB(p - e.yxy),

mapB(p + e.yyx) - mapB(p - e.yyx)

));

}

vec3 nOcean(vec2 xz) {

float e = 0.12;

float h = ocean(xz);

return normalize(vec3(

h - ocean(xz + vec2(e, 0.0)),

e,

h - ocean(xz + vec2(0.0, e))

));

}

// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Lightning + sky

// ------------------------------------------------------------

float lightning(float t) {

float c = t * 0.4;

float id = floor(c);

float f = fract(c);

float r = hash12(vec2(id, 7.3));

if (r > 0.87) {

float b = exp(-f * 8.0) * (0.7 + 0.3 * sin(t * 50.0));

b += 0.4 * exp(-abs(f - 0.1) * 28.0) * step(0.4, hash12(vec2(id, 2.2)));

return clamp(b, 0.0, 1.4);

}

return 0.0;

}

vec3 sky(vec3 rd, float fl) {

float y = max(rd.y, 0.0);

vec3 col = mix(vec3(0.012, 0.018, 0.03),

vec3(0.055, 0.06, 0.075),

exp(-y * 3.0));

col = mix(col, vec3(0.07, 0.06, 0.055), exp(-abs(rd.y) * 8.0) * 0.6);

vec2 cuv = rd.xz / (rd.y + 0.15);

float clouds = smoothstep(0.3, 0.75, fbm(cuv * 1.6 + time * 0.03));

col = mix(col, vec3(0.025, 0.028, 0.04), clouds * 0.85);

col += fl * vec3(0.4, 0.45, 0.75) * (0.3 + 0.7 * clouds);

return col;

}

// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Main

// ------------------------------------------------------------

void main() {

vec2 uv = (gl_FragCoord.xy - 0.5 * resolution.xy) / resolution.y;

// mouse look (optional)

vec2 mo = mouse / max(resolution, vec2(1.0));

mo = mo * 2.0 - 1.0;

if (length(mouse) < 0.5) mo = vec2(0.0);

float t = time;

float fl = lightning(t);

// cinematic path

float path = t * 2.9;

float h0 = 8.5 + 4.0 * sin(t * 0.22);

vec3 ro = vec3(path,

h0 + ocean(vec2(path, 0.0)) * 0.35,

7.5 * sin(t * 0.14));

vec3 ta = ro + vec3(12.0,

-1.0 + 5.0 * sin(t * 0.16) + mo.y * 4.0,

5.0 * cos(t * 0.12) + mo.x * 6.0);

// thunder shake

ro.xy += (hash22(vec2(floor(t * 7.0), 1.1)) - 0.5) * fl * 0.3;

vec3 ww = normalize(ta - ro);

vec3 uu = normalize(cross(ww, vec3(0.0, 1.0, 0.0)));

vec3 vv = cross(uu, ww);

vec3 rd = normalize(uv.x * uu + uv.y * vv + 1.3 * ww);

// ---- raymarch (lighter) ----

float trav = 0.0;

float tmax = 160.0;

vec3 p;

for (int i = 0; i < 90; i++) {

p = ro + rd * trav;

float d = map(p);

if (d < 0.0015 * trav || trav > tmax) break;

trav += d * 0.8;

}

vec3 col = sky(rd, fl);

if (trav < tmax) {

p = ro + rd * trav;

float dB = mapB(p);

float h = ocean(p.xz);

bool water = (p.y - h) < dB * 0.9 + 0.02;

if (water) {

// ---- WATER ----

vec3 n = nOcean(p.xz);

// foam

float eps = 0.15;

float steep = length(vec2(

ocean(p.xz + vec2(eps, 0.0)) - h,

ocean(p.xz + vec2(0.0, eps)) - h)) / eps;

float foam = smoothstep(0.55, 1.4, steep);

foam = max(foam, smoothstep(1.6, 3.2, h + 0.6 * fbm(p.xz * 0.3 + t * 0.2)));

foam = clamp(foam, 0.0, 1.0);

float fres = pow(1.0 - max(dot(-rd, n), 0.0), 4.0);

// cheap reflection (sky only)

vec3 refl = sky(reflect(rd, n), fl);

vec3 waterCol = mix(vec3(0.005, 0.03, 0.04),

vec3(0.02, 0.055, 0.06),

max(dot(-rd, n), 0.0));

waterCol = mix(waterCol, vec3(0.7, 0.76, 0.85), foam * 0.9);

vec3 L = normalize(vec3(0.4, 0.8, 0.35));

float diff = max(dot(n, L), 0.0);

float spec = pow(max(dot(reflect(-L, n), -rd), 0.0), 70.0);

float lspec = fl * max(dot(n, vec3(0.0, 1.0, 0.0)), 0.0) * 1.2;

col = waterCol * (0.25 + diff * 0.4 + fl * 0.3);

col += vec3(0.9, 0.95, 1.0) * (spec * 0.8 + lspec * 0.5);

col = mix(col, refl, fres * (0.65 + 0.3 * foam));

col += foam * foam * 0.25;

} else {

// ---- STONE ----

vec3 n = nBuild(p);

vec2 id = floor(p.xz / 28.0);

vec3 albedo = vec3(0.12, 0.11, 0.105);

albedo *= 0.7 + 0.45 * hash12(id + floor(p.y * 0.2));

// algae lower down

albedo = mix(vec3(0.04, 0.06, 0.045), albedo,

smoothstep(-1.0, 20.0, p.y) * 0.9 + 0.1);

float wet = clamp(exp(-(p.y - h) * 0.5), 0.0, 1.0);

albedo *= mix(1.0, 0.3, wet);

// glowing windows

float fy = fract(p.y * 0.38);

float fxz = min(abs(fract(p.x * 0.5) - 0.5),

abs(fract(p.z * 0.5) - 0.5));

float win = step(0.22, fy) * step(fy, 0.7) * step(fxz, 0.09);

float lit = step(0.5, hash12(floor(p.xy * vec2(0.5, 0.38)) + id));

lit *= 0.7 + 0.3 * sin(t * 3.0 + hash12(id) * 10.0);

vec3 glowHue = mix(vec3(1.0, 0.5, 0.15),

vec3(0.3, 0.8, 1.15),

hash12(id + 5.0));

vec3 emission = glowHue * win * lit * 2.6;

// top lantern glow

emission += vec3(1.0, 0.7, 0.3) *

smoothstep(24.0, 40.0, p.y) * 0.35 * (0.5 + 0.5 * fl);

vec3 L = normalize(vec3(0.35, 0.8, 0.4));

float dif = max(dot(n, L), 0.0);

float amb = 0.18 + 0.16 * n.y + fl * 0.55;

float spec = pow(max(dot(reflect(-L, n), -rd), 0.0), mix(16.0, 40.0, wet))

* 0.4 * mix(0.2, 0.8, wet);

// cheap AO

float ao = clamp(mapB(p + n * 1.0) / 1.0, 0.4, 1.0);

col = albedo * (amb + dif * 0.65) * ao + vec3(0.95) * spec + emission;

}

// fog

float fog = 1.0 - exp(-trav * trav * 0.00004);

fog = clamp(fog + 0.12 * (1.0 - exp(-trav * 0.015)), 0.0, 1.0);

vec3 fogC = mix(vec3(0.04, 0.045, 0.06), sky(rd, fl) * 0.5, 0.4);

fogC += fl * 0.06;

col = mix(col, fogC, fog);

}

// ---- post ----

// rain

vec2 ruv = uv * vec2(48.0, 20.0) + vec2(0.0, -t * 25.0);

float rain = smoothstep(0.93, 0.995, noise(floor(ruv)));

ruv = uv * vec2(80.0, 35.0) + vec2(12.0, -t * 38.0);

rain += 0.5 * smoothstep(0.94, 0.998, noise(floor(ruv)));

col += rain * (0.1 + 0.05 * fl) * vec3(0.6, 0.68, 0.8);

// screen flash

col += fl * 0.07;

// bloom-ish

col += max(col - 0.7, 0.0) * 0.4;

// grade + tone

col = col * 1.15 / (1.0 + col * 0.5);

col = mix(col, col * vec3(0.82, 0.93, 1.15), 0.35);

// vignette

vec2 v = gl_FragCoord.xy / resolution.xy;

float vig = v.x * v.y * (1.0 - v.x) * (1.0 - v.y);

col *= mix(0.55, 1.0, pow(vig * 14.0, 0.25));

col = pow(max(col, 0.0), vec3(0.92));

gl_FragColor = vec4(col, 1.0);

}

items.title: Gemma 4 Technical Report: "We introduce Gemma 4, a new generation of open-weight, natively multimoda...
items.content:
Gemma 4 Technical Report: "We introduce Gemma 4, a new generation of open-weight, natively multimodal language models in the Gemma model family. Designed to advance compute efficiency and reasoning, the Gemma 4 model suite features dense and Mixture-of-Experts architectures, ranging from 2.3B to 31B parameters. Alongside improved vision and audio encoders for all model sizes, we propose a unified, encoder-free architecture for our 12B model, which ingests raw audio and image patches. Furthermore, we integrate a thinking mode, enabling Gemma models to generate reasoning traces prior to responding. We improve inference speed, memory, and compute efficiency, as well as long-context abilities through critical design choices. Gemma 4 establishes a leap in performance across STEM, multimodal, and long-context benchmarks, and rivals larger, frontier open models in human-rated tasks."

https://t.co/Hz1Y2qPqJ3

items.title: OpenAI just rolled out GPT-Live, its next-gen voice model that makes talking to AI "feel much more l...
items.content:
OpenAI just rolled out GPT-Live, its next-gen voice model that makes talking to AI "feel much more like having a real conversation". 

The demo showed off a fast, super smooth back-and-forth without the awkward interruptions or pauses of older voice models. 

GPT-Live is a "full-duplex" architecture, which processes inputs continuously instead of predecessors that strung together models and used turn-based conversation.

GPT-Live is also able to delegate work to other models, allowing it to leverage top systems in the background during conversations. 

Another demo showed off real-time translation capabilities, with GPT-Live able to listen, process, and output at the same time.

Voice conversations in ChatGPT just got a big upgrade.

OpenAI just rolled out GPT-Live, its next-gen voice model that makes talking to AI "feel much more like having a real conversation".

The demo showed off a fast, super smooth back-and-forth without the awkward interruptions or pauses of older voice models.

GPT-Live is a "full-duplex" architecture, which processes inputs continuously instead of predecessors that strung together models and used turn-based conversation.

GPT-Live is also able to delegate work to other models, allowing it to leverage top systems in the background during conversations.

Another demo showed off real-time translation capabilities, with GPT-Live able to listen, process, and output at the same time.

Voice conversations in ChatGPT just got a big upgrade.

items.title: This world model is impressing many, including me. Longer video length means that the companies are ...
items.content:
This world model is impressing many, including me. Longer video length means that the companies are figuring out how to make the world models more efficient. 

Now, in 18 months, what do you think that leads to? How about two video streams, one going to each eye in a pair of glasses? And let's see the quality go way up.

As impressive as this is, it's still very early in world model history.

items.title: Well agree to both disagree and agree. Maybe you have done a lot in psychology and I agree that bein...
items.content:
Well agree to both disagree and agree. Maybe you have done a lot in psychology and I agree that being pushed to learn new things or techniques also helps both athletes and average person improve. However, repetition  is also key as shown by Lee, Swanson & Hall (1991), Physical Therapy – “What Is Repeated in a Repetition? Effects of Practice Conditions on Motor Skill Acquisition”
and Shea et al. (2000); Lee & Genovese (1988) – Distribution of practice  plus Even well‑practiced movements benefit from repetition (Journal of Neurophysiology, 2022) .

More to the point at the very elite level academic studies are difficult as the sample size is so small and hence hearing from the likes of Henry, etc how they attained the very top level performance is where I disagree with you. And I fear the UK education system - which I have experienced in recent years too, as a mature student, has dropped rigorous standards. To be fair the UK was not so good at languages, maths or code at the mass level of society for sometime and having attended schools (dance, martial arts and academic) outside of the UK I observed that they push repeating practice a lot more vs my observation in the UK.

items.content_html:
Well agree to both disagree and agree. Maybe you have done a lot in psychology and I agree that being pushed to learn new things or techniques also helps both athletes and average person improve. However, repetition is also key as shown by Lee, Swanson & Hall (1991), Physical Therapy – “What Is Repeated in a Repetition? Effects of Practice Conditions on Motor Skill Acquisition”

and Shea et al. (2000); Lee & Genovese (1988) – Distribution of practice plus Even well‑practiced movements benefit from repetition (Journal of Neurophysiology, 2022) .

More to the point at the very elite level academic studies are difficult as the sample size is so small and hence hearing from the likes of Henry, etc how they attained the very top level performance is where I disagree with you. And I fear the UK education system - which I have experienced in recent years too, as a mature student, has dropped rigorous standards. To be fair the UK was not so good at languages, maths or code at the mass level of society for sometime and having attended schools (dance, martial arts and academic) outside of the UK I observed that they push repeating practice a lot more vs my observation in the UK.

items.title:
AuroraGPT - Built by Scars of Frontline

The theory is nothing. AuroraGPT advances scientific discov...
items.content:
AuroraGPT - Built by Scars of Frontline

The theory is nothing. AuroraGPT advances scientific discovery by building generalizable, foundation-scale large-language models across multiple scientific domains, including physics, chemistry, mathematics, and material sciences. In a world dominated by theoretical models and theoritic ideals, AuroraGPT was never born from theoretical laboratory conditions. AuroraGPT forged its foundation in the real-world challenges of exascale computing. Traditional machine learning models operate in narrowly defined, well-structured environments. However, AuroraGPT goes beyond static datasets into domains where mathematics, programming, and extreme complexity dominate. Its architecture generalizes across scientific disciplines, enabling adaptation to evolving scientific data. It also enables faster, scalable, predictive modeling that can adapt to changing scientific data — something conventional models struggle to accomplish at meaningful resolution or exascale computing.

Achieving these objectives required AuroraGPT to survive in the harshest computational environments ever encountered—the early volatile stages of the Aurora Supercomputer. Building at exascale demanded fault-tolerant training algorithms capable of surviving node dropouts, managing inconsistent interconnect bandwidths, and recovering from partial model convergence failures. AuroraGPT’s foundation large language model scaling strategy involved distributing training across 60,000 GPUs while dynamically re-optimizing tensor parallelism, complex mathematics, data parallelism, and pipeline parallelism in fluctuating infrastructure stability. AuroraGPT engineered its resilience mechanisms in direct response to real-world node failures, memory bottlenecks, and system fragmentation — scars from computational battles that ultimately forged a new class of singularity-resilient AI infrastructure.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

References 

Argonne National Laboratory. (n.d.). AuroraGPT: Built by the scars of battle. Argonne National Laboratory. Retrieved April 26, 2025, from https://t.co/paRrpIy5oh

Stevens, R., & Foster, I. (n.d.). AuroraGPT: A large language-scale foundation model for advancing science. Argonne Leadership Computing Facility. Retrieved April 26, 2025, from https://t.co/JIZOoz7WjP

Rosenberg, T. (2023, January 31). The computer that will change everything. Chicago Magazine. Retrieved April 26, 2025, from https://t.co/1Duk8wJ6ey

Thakur, R. (2024, August 5). AuroraGPT: A large-scale foundation model from Argonne Laboratory. Zenodo. Retrieved April 26, 2025, from https://t.co/Tcqp4kcIZp

items.title:
Agentic AI workloads are fundamentally different:

→ They're continuous and looping—not occasional t...
items.content:
Agentic AI workloads are fundamentally different:

→ They're continuous and looping—not occasional tasks.
→ Agents interact directly with enterprise systems, files, and live data.
→ That introduces new governance and access control considerations.

That's why @HPE infrastructure and the @NVIDIA AI software stack are designed to help organizations support enterprise-scale Agentic AI.

items.title:
Enterprise AI challenges are not always about model accuracy.
In many operational environments, the ...
items.content:
Enterprise AI challenges are not always about model accuracy.
In many operational environments, the issue is timing.
 
The factory has already made the defect. The robot already moved. The process already drifted. When response times lag behind operations, even strong AI systems can lose impact.
 
A thread on latency, edge AI, and operational responsiveness.
 
@TMobileBusiness Partner

items.title: If you add up all the rides in any autonomous vehicle, whether it's from Zoox, Waymo, Tesla, or any ...
items.content:
If you add up all the rides in any autonomous vehicle, whether it's from Zoox, Waymo, Tesla, or any of the Chinese companies, it comes down to less than 10 million people so far out of 8 billion people. 

Most people haven't even started considering such a thing, and that's what makes it so interesting as a marketing challenge: how do you get everybody on board quickly?
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer notes fewer than 10 million people worldwide have taken autonomous vehicle rides from Zoox, Waymo, Tesla, or Chinese firms, framing mass adoption as a marketing challenge.
items.reasoning: Offers a concrete (if rough) adoption stat and market framing for AV deployment; low engagement but more substantive than his other AV posts.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer notes fewer than 10 million people worldwide have taken autonomous vehicle rides from Zoox, Waymo, Tesla, or Chinese firms, framing mass adoption as a marketing challenge.
items.content_html:
If you add up all the rides in any autonomous vehicle, whether it's from Zoox, Waymo, Tesla, or any of the Chinese companies, it comes down to less than 10 million people so far out of 8 billion people.

Most people haven't even started considering such a thing, and that's what makes it so interesting as a marketing challenge: how do you get everybody on board quickly?

items.title:
Red Memory Pressure: Your Computer Needs More RAM. 

Though, at the outset it might appear a human f...
items.content:
Red Memory Pressure: Your Computer Needs More RAM. 

Though, at the outset it might appear a human failure, running high speed tweet posting algorithms while parallelly loading thousands of research papers triggered a cascading memory exhaustion event on macOS. Each PDF rendering consumed 300–1,000 MB of RAM. Each network request from the tweet automation script created URLSession objects that macOS couldn't garbage collect fast enough. When physical RAM was saturated, the kernel began swap thrashing writing gigabytes of data to the SSD at 10–100× lower speed than RAM until two machines became unresponsive! Research using Zotero's PDF viewer documented that opening the first PDF alone consumed over 1 GB of RAM, with each subsequent one adding 300+ MB. Opening 100+ PDFs in Safari was enough to crash even 2026 MacBook Neo hardware. A CMU study found that 25% of participants reported their browser or computer crashed specifically due to having too many research papers open. 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #NLProc #LLM #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

https://t.co/v90KocmPHQ

items.title:
Here’s what that looks like in practice:
 
Manufacturing → AI identifies a defect, but the response ...
items.content:
Here’s what that looks like in practice:
 
Manufacturing → AI identifies a defect, but the response loop is delayed → More flawed products continue down the line → Waste and rework increase
Logistics → Robots and workflows wait for updated instructions → Small delays compound across operations
 
Industrial environments → Sensors identify anomalies, but delayed responses can affect operational continuity
 
The challenge is not always the model itself. Sometimes it’s the timing of the response.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Gives examples of latency harming manufacturing, logistics, and industrial operations when AI responses lag.
items.reasoning: Illustrative but generic sponsored content with negligible engagement.
items.summary_html: Gives examples of latency harming manufacturing, logistics, and industrial operations when AI responses lag.
items.content_html:
Here’s what that looks like in practice:

Manufacturing → AI identifies a defect, but the response loop is delayed → More flawed products continue down the line → Waste and rework increase

Logistics → Robots and workflows wait for updated instructions → Small delays compound across operations

Industrial environments → Sensors identify anomalies, but delayed responses can affect operational continuity

The challenge is not always the model itself. Sometimes it’s the timing of the response.

items.title:
For years, the focus was: Can AI do this?
 
Now the better question is: Can AI respond fast enough t...
items.content:
For years, the focus was: Can AI do this?
 
Now the better question is: Can AI respond fast enough to influence what happens next?
 
Models are improving quickly. Access to AI is expanding.
 
That means operational responsiveness is becoming more important across manufacturing, logistics, and industrial environments.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Argues the key question is shifting from whether AI can do a task to whether it can respond fast enough to influence outcomes.
items.reasoning: Reasonable reframing but sponsored and generic with no engagement.
items.summary_html: Argues the key question is shifting from whether AI can do a task to whether it can respond fast enough to influence outcomes.
items.content_html:
For years, the focus was: Can AI do this?

Now the better question is: Can AI respond fast enough to influence what happens next?

Models are improving quickly. Access to AI is expanding.

That means operational responsiveness is becoming more important across manufacturing, logistics, and industrial environments.

AlphaSignal Workshop - 5.5M wasted queries a year. Is your agent one of them?

If your agent’s web s...
items.content:
AlphaSignal Workshop - 5.5M wasted queries a year. Is your agent one of them?

If your agent’s web search is just an LLM + search API, it’s probably slower, more expensive, and less accurate than it should be.

July 28th @ 10a PT, @seltz_ai founder Antonio Mallia (built web search for Alexa) runs a live side-by-side naive setup vs. real index, same queries, plus a self-test you can run on your own stack same day.

Reserve your seat for this virtual workshop today: https://t.co/6vsD0pwJax

Because in environments like manufacturing, logistics, and industrial automation, even small delays can affect efficiency, precision, and responsiveness.
 
Edge environments can help reduce the gap between insight and operational action.

items.title:
The goal is not to replace the cloud.
It’s to support decision-making closer to where data is genera...
items.content:
The goal is not to replace the cloud.
It’s to support decision-making closer to where data is generated.
 
That’s where edge AI environments can help reduce latency and support more responsive operations.
 
This is exactly what Edge Control from @TMobileBusiness is designed to support.
 
Edge Control routes data closer to operations and supports low-latency environments for time-sensitive workloads.
 
Teams can also manage deployments through T-Platform, T-Mobile’s unified dashboard for advanced network solutions.

items.title: The @BBC is participating in @UniversalPics's damage control. What's interesting is that the BBC is ...
items.content:
The @BBC is participating in @UniversalPics's damage control. What's interesting is that the BBC is a public broadcast service, paid for by the British people out of their own pockets. So, the British people paid for this, then the BBC also took money from Universal and kept it, and then fed this propaganda to the British people.

And this is the BBC's approach to all news, including politics. Especailly politics. I don't understand how the British public isn't pissed. Apparently, the BBC managed to brainwash them before they could get pissed.

items.title: @OmnipotentCEO The real goal is to not need seat belts at all. If cars never wreck, why do you need ...
items.content:
@OmnipotentCEO The real goal is to not need seat belts at all. If cars never wreck, why do you need them?

But that's decades away. So yeah, we should put some more engineering effort into seatbelts and making them more comfortable and better in crashes and stuff like that.

But that's decades away. So yeah, we should put some more engineering effort into seatbelts and making them more comfortable and better in crashes and stuff like that.

items.title:
Total BS. 
France is just about as ethnically diverse as the US and has similar levels of immigratio...
items.content:
Total BS. 
France is just about as ethnically diverse as the US and has similar levels of immigration (14-15% of immigrants).

Also, claiming that ethnic diversity causes a lowering of life expectancy is exposing your racism. 

[Note: France does not recognize the notion of race. That's an American construct]

items.title: I've been here 19 years, and my life is so much better because of it. I've gotten into San Quentin P...
items.content:
I've been here 19 years, and my life is so much better because of it. I've gotten into San Quentin Prison, onto the decks of aircraft carriers, into car races, and all sorts of other fun things because of this place. Plus, I've gotten jobs, friends, and so many other things out of it.

GoPro went public after they published a single video that got viewed hundreds of millions of times. So I've seen the power of media to change perception. 

Welcome.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer reflects on 19 years of benefits from a platform and the power of media, citing GoPro going public after a viral video.
items.reasoning: Personal reflection on media influence with minor tech-adjacent relevance.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer reflects on 19 years of benefits from a platform and the power of media, citing GoPro going public after a viral video.
items.content_html:
I've been here 19 years, and my life is so much better because of it. I've gotten into San Quentin Prison, onto the decks of aircraft carriers, into car races, and all sorts of other fun things because of this place. Plus, I've gotten jobs, friends, and so many other things out of it.

GoPro went public after they published a single video that got viewed hundreds of millions of times. So I've seen the power of media to change perception.

Welcome.

@RealRoseGoblin Ha, that's funny. My wife and I met in the former coroner's office of Palo Alto, so maybe we're into that kind of thing. 

Anyways, we're headed home to San Jose. Sorry we missed you! Yeah, L.A. traffic is interesting, but autonomous cars make it actually more entertaining.

items.content_html:
@RealRoseGoblin Ha, that's funny. My wife and I met in the former coroner's office of Palo Alto, so maybe we're into that kind of thing.

Anyways, we're headed home to San Jose. Sorry we missed you! Yeah, L.A. traffic is interesting, but autonomous cars make it actually more entertaining.
